Sub-theme name: Prior  – Consent is Obtained Before Project Decisions Are Made
Descriptor: Consent must be sought early in the project development process—before decisions are finalized or activities begin. Communities must be engaged at the initial stages of planning and have ample time to discuss, evaluate, and decide without being rushed. FPIC should not be applied retroactively to justify actions already taken.
Indicators: Were you and your community consulted about this project before any decisions were made or activities began? (https://www.notion.so/Were-you-and-your-community-consulted-about-this-project-before-any-decisions-were-made-or-activitie-34060bfb014e81b1b7c8d2b95d3007a8?pvs=21), Was your community given enough time to discuss the project internally, seek advice, and make a decision before being asked for consent? Sub-theme name: Informed  – Access to Full, Accurate, and Understandable Information
Descriptor: Communities must have access to all relevant details about the project, including its objectives, risks, benefits, impacts, and long-term implications. Information should be shared in culturally appropriate formats and languages, ensuring that all groups—including women, elders, and marginalized members—can understand and engage meaningfully. IPLCs must also have the opportunity to seek independent advice or verification.
Indicators: Did you receive complete and accurate information about the project — including its objectives, risks, benefits, and long-term implications — in a language and format you could understand? Sub-theme name: Consent  – Right to Accept, Reject, or Set Conditions
Descriptor: Communities must have the power to approve, reject, or set conditions for project activities affecting them. Consent must be an ongoing process, allowing communities to withdraw or renegotiate agreements as circumstances change. Genuine consent means that decisions are respected, and project proponents cannot proceed without clear and collective agreement from the affected community.
Indicators: Did your community have a genuine right to reject the project or set conditions, and did you feel that rejection was a real and respected option? (https://www.notion.so/Did-your-community-have-a-genuine-right-to-reject-the-project-or-set-conditions-and-did-you-feel-th-34060bfb014e813abd23e59f8bac4935?pvs=21), Was your community’s decision regarding the project — whether to approve, reject, or set conditions — respected and upheld by the project proponents? Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Improved Reliability of Essential Public Services
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance, significance, and lived value of improved reliability, functionality, and sustained accessibility of essential public services - including education, healthcare, and water.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Increased Village-Level Economic Activity
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of increased village-level economic activity associated with the presence of salaried staff and expanded service provision.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Improved School Attendance and Participation
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of improved school attendance and consistent participation associated with school feeding and related educational support.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Reduced Caregiving Burden
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of reduced or redistributed caregiving responsibilities within households, and the extent to which changes in care burden alter how time is allocated.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Increased Household Income
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of increases in household income resulting from expanded income-generating activities, including those enabled through access to community-based savings and credit mechanisms.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Increased Reliability of Farming Conditions
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of increased reliability of farming conditions, including greater confidence in seasonal timing and crop yields.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Importance and Value of Improved Household Health
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the importance and value of improvements in personal or household health associated with increased access to preventative and local healthcare services, including earlier treatment, improved maternal health, and reduced severity of illness.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Fairness of Benefit Distribution Within the Village
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of whether the benefits associated with an intervention are distributed equitably among households or community members, including whether any groups are perceived to be favoured or disadvantaged.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Inclusiveness and Representation in Village Decision-Making
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of whether decision-making processes related to an intervention are inclusive, representative, and allow meaningful participation across different groups.
This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of the integrity, reliability, and good faith of those responsible for implementing the intervention.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Continuity of the Intervention and Its Benefits
Descriptor: This construct captures stakeholder perceptions of whether the intervention, its associated benefits, and the rules governing it are likely to continue over time without interruption, sudden change, or withdrawal.

Sub-theme name: Perceived Social Burdens and Tensions Associated with the Intervention
Descriptor: This sub-theme captures stakeholder perceptions of social burdens, restrictions, or tensions associated with an intervention, including concerns about conflict, exclusion, or negative social consequences.

This subtheme refers to the construction, rehabilitation, expansion, or upgrading of physical facilities and systems that support the delivery of essential social services, particularly in sectors such as education, health, and water. It focuses on improving the availability, functionality, and accessibility of infrastructure needed for people to obtain these services, and on creating the conditions for better service access, use, and wellbeing outcomes.

Sub-theme name: Caregiver Support for Learning at Home
Descriptor: Covers the home learning environment, homework support, reading with children, and creation of conditions conducive to study. The most direct caregiver influence on learner attainment and consistently the strongest predictor of outcomes in the parental engagement literature. Captures home-based support as a distinct and critical engagement mechanism.

Sub-theme name: Caregiver Attitudes and Beliefs about Education
Descriptor: Covers caregiver values, aspirations, and expectations for their child's education. Caregiver beliefs about the value of education are stronger predictors of learner persistence than structural engagement behaviours. Captures the attitudinal preconditions for sustained engagement rather than the behaviours themselves.

Covers structural, economic, cultural, and social barriers preventing caregivers from engaging, including work schedules, distance, gender norms, and language. A complementary diagnostic subtheme rather than a standalone outcome subtheme. Essential for interpreting low scores on any other caregiver engagement indicator and for informing programme design.
Indicators: Are there any practical difficulties, such as work commitments, distance from the school, or cost of transport, that make it hard for you to be involved in your child's schooling? (https://www.notion.so/Are-there-any-practical-difficulties-such-as-work-commitments-distance-from-the-school-or-cost-of-33f60bfb014e8142a31bef233d5238c2?pvs=21), What are those difficulties, and how do they affect your involvement? (https://www.notion.so/Can-you-describe-those-expectations-and-how-they-affect-your-involvement-in-your-child-s-schooling-33f60bfb014e8170bb64d5d628f1894c?pvs=21)

Sub-theme name: Caregiver Confidence and Capacity to Engage
Descriptor: Covers caregiver self-efficacy in supporting learning, including literacy, numeracy, and confidence to interact with school staff. Captures programme effects on caregiver empowerment as a condition for engagement, particularly relevant in low-literacy contexts where willingness to engage may exist but skills or confidence are barriers.

Covers caregiver mental health, stress levels, and personal resilience as determinants of the quality and consistency of care and learning support they can provide. A caregiver who is overwhelmed, burnt out, or experiencing chronic stress cannot engage meaningfully with their child's education regardless of their knowledge, attitudes, or skills. This subtheme captures the personal resource dimension of caregiver empowerment and is a documented upstream determinant of child learning outcomes in adverse childhood experience and family resilience literature.

Descriptor: Covers the degree to which caregivers feel a sense of personal agency, voice, and capacity to make informed decisions that affect their child's education and wellbeing. Distinct from task-specific confidence in homework support and from peer support participation. Captures whether caregivers see themselves as active agents in their child's educational journey rather than passive recipients of school and programme decisions. Grounded in Freire's critical pedagogy and Malhotra and Schuler's empowerment framework.

Distinct from peer leadership, which is relational and peer-directed, and from community service, which is activity-based. Civic engagement captures whether young people are developing as active citizens who can navigate and influence formal civic structures. Grounded in Youniss, Flanagan, and Checkoway's civic development literature.

Sub-theme name: Recognition and Visibility of Youth Contribution
Descriptor: Covers whether youth contributions, ideas, and leadership are formally acknowledged, made visible to the broader community, and positioned as credible and valued by adults and institutions. Recognition is distinct from contribution as a behaviour and captures the structural and relational conditions that sustain youth engagement over time. Whether youth feel seen and valued as contributors is a documented predictor of civic identity development, sustained participation, and resilience against marginalisation, particularly for young people from underrepresented or excluded groups.
Indicators: In the past 12 months, has an adult, organisation, or community figure formally recognised or acknowledged something you contributed or led, for example through a certificate, a public mention, an award, or an official thank you? (https://www.notion.so/In-the-past-12-months-has-an-adult-organisation-or-community-figure-formally-recognised-or-acknow-33f60bfb014e810ca495e4fbea9e59f2?pvs=21), Who recognised you, what was it for, and how was it recognised? (https://www.notion.so/In-the-past-12-months-was-something-you-did-or-led-shared-with-or-presented-to-people-outside-your--33f60bfb014e813e9564e4e36b36c541?pvs=21), What was shared, who saw it, and where or how was it shown? (https://www.notion.so/What-was-shared-who-saw-it-and-where-or-how-was-it-shown-33f60bfb014e819ca7dcc81ec46224ea?pvs=21), How much do you agree with this statement: The adults and organisations around me notice when young people contribute something meaningful. (https://www.notion.so/How-much-do-you-agree-with-this-statement-When-young-people-in-this-programme-lead-something-their-33f60bfb014e81afbcdae668cea8e5cb?pvs=21), How much do you agree with this statement: I feel that my own contributions and efforts are seen and appreciated by the people around me. (https://www.notion.so/How-much-do-you-agree-with-this-statement-I-feel-that-my-own-contributions-and-efforts-are-seen-and-33f60bfb014e812eaf71d342515a4c09?pvs=21)

Distinct from partner alignment, which concerns strategic and evidential coordination. Cross-sector coordination and referral captures the operational interface between systems at the point of delivery.

Descriptor: Covers whether programme partners share data, jointly collect monitoring information, and coordinate data use to avoid duplication and build a shared picture of what is happening across the system. Distinct from partner alignment, which concerns strategic coordination. Captures the operational systems and practices through which monitoring information flows across actors, enabling a coherent shared evidence base rather than fragmented parallel monitoring efforts.

(https://www.notion.so/Which-requirement-was-not-met-and-what-was-the-reason-34060bfb014e81e0986cf4926029ca83?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Covers whether programme outcomes are equitably distributed across defined equity dimensions including gender, disability, socioeconomic background, ethnicity, geographic location, and age band. A monitoring and analysis construct rather than a programme activity construct. The ultimate test of whether the programme's equity commitments translate into equitable results, requiring systematic outcome disaggregation and gap analysis.

Covers the degree to which girls, women, and gender-diverse young people participate equitably in programme activities, have equal voice in decision-making, and experience gender-equitable norms and attitudes in learning and community settings. Distinct from targeting and barrier reduction, which concerns access, and from disaggregated outcome equity, which concerns outcome distribution. Captures whether equitable participation is achieved in practice and whether gender norms are shifting.

A perceptual equity construct distinct from structural access and from outcome equity. Captures the lived experience of inclusion, which is the most meaningful test of whether structural equity measures are working in practice.

Sub-theme name: Material and Physical Home Learning Conditions
Descriptor: Covers the physical and material conditions within the household that either enable or suppress a child's capacity to engage with learning at home. Distinct from caregiver behaviours, which are covered under Caregiver Support for Learning at Home. Captures the structural material infrastructure of the home environment, including study space, lighting, learning materials, and freedom from competing household demands during study time.

Descriptor: Covers whether the programme is transparent and accountable to the learners, caregivers, and communities it serves, including how it communicates results, invites and responds to feedback, and handles complaints. A downward accountability construct distinct from community engagement, which concerns involvement, and from evidence use, which is internal. Captures whether the programme reports back to those it serves and responds to concerns in a timely and trustworthy way.
Indicators: How much do you agree with this statement: This programme regularly shares information with communities about what it has achieved and what it has learned. (https://www.notion.so/How-much-do-you-agree-with-this-statement-This-programme-regularly-shares-information-with-communit-34060bfb014e81ef824beaa967bff768?pvs=21), How much do you agree with this statement: When communities raise concerns with this programme, those concerns are followed up and acted on. Descriptor: Covers whether the programme identifies learners who have fallen behind expected learning levels and provides structured catch-up or remediation support to close those gaps. Distinct from foundational learning experiences, which concerns ongoing learning delivery for all learners, and from attendance and enrolment continuity, which concerns structural participation. Captures the targeted diagnostic and recovery response when continuity has broken down for specific learners.

(https://www.notion.so/In-the-current-school-year-were-you-identified-as-needing-extra-support-or-catch-up-help-with-your--34060bfb014e81efb3b9ce5fd749a080?pvs=21), Were you offered extra sessions or support to help you catch up? Descriptor: Covers whether learners remain enrolled and attending consistently across the school year and between school years, capturing the structural participation dimension of learning continuity. Distinct from foundational learning experiences, which concerns learning quality, and from learning loss identification and recovery, which concerns remediation after disruption. Attendance and enrolment continuity is the structural prerequisite for all learning progression outcomes.
Indicators: In the current school term, how often do you attend your scheduled learning sessions? (https://www.notion.so/In-the-current-school-term-how-often-do-you-attend-your-scheduled-learning-sessions-34060bfb014e811789dcdfb281181d97?pvs=21), What are the main reasons you have missed some sessions? (https://www.notion.so/Were-you-enrolled-in-this-programme-or-school-last-year-34060bfb014e81bbbf84e4f901881004?pvs=21), Was there a period when you were not in school or this programme? What happened, and what brought you back? (https://www.notion.so/What-would-you-do-34060bfb014e81aaa6dbeee0d419b786?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Covers whether learners successfully navigate key educational transitions, including from primary to secondary, from out-of-school to enrolled, or from non-formal to formal education, and whether the programme provides structured transition support. Distinct from attendance and enrolment continuity, which concerns within-level participation, and from foundational learning experiences, which concerns within-level learning quality. Captures movement between educational levels and systems, a documented high dropout risk point.
Indicators: After completing your most recent school year or level, did you enrol in the next level of education or training? (https://www.notion.so/After-completing-your-most-recent-school-year-or-level-did-you-enrol-in-the-next-level-of-education-34060bfb014e816fa365d6c3fc0a85f0?pvs=21), What prevented you from enrolling in the next level? (https://www.notion.so/How-much-do-you-agree-with-this-statement-At-my-new-level-of-education-or-training-I-feel-adequate-34060bfb014e810685b8c96d7b5d7841?pvs=21)

Descriptor: Covers whether young people feel capable of making informed decisions about their educational and livelihood futures, have engaged in deliberate decision-making processes, and feel confident taking the steps required to pursue their chosen pathways. An agency and self-efficacy construct distinct from pathway knowledge, which is cognitive, and from institutional navigation, which is operational. Captures whether young people believe they can shape their own futures and are actively doing so.

Descriptor: Covers whether young people are able to access and navigate the institutions, services, and referral systems needed to pursue their chosen education and livelihood pathways, including applying to institutions, accessing financial support, and completing required enrolment processes. The operational navigation dimension distinct from pathway knowledge, which concerns what is known, and from decision-making self-efficacy, which concerns the confidence to choose. Captures whether young people can complete the practical steps between pathway decision and pathway entry.

Descriptor: Covers whether young people have accurate, sufficient, and accessible knowledge of the education and livelihood pathways available to them, including entry requirements, costs, timelines, application processes, and sources of support. The information precondition for informed pathway navigation. Distinct from decision-making self-efficacy, which concerns the agentic dimension, and from institutional navigation, which concerns access rather than knowledge.

Descriptor: Covers the positive psychological resources that enable young people to engage actively and constructively with learning and life, including hope, sense of purpose, motivation, positive self-concept, and optimism. Distinct from emotional safety and stability, which concerns freedom from threat, and from resilience and coping, which concerns managing adversity. Captures the presence of constructive psychological assets rather than the absence of distress, representing the positive pole of the psychosocial wellbeing spectrum.

Descriptor: Covers whether young people have positive, supportive, and trusting peer relationships within the programme environment, including friendships, peer support networks, and social belonging to the peer group. Distinct from broader institutional belonging under Equity and Inclusion and from interpersonal safety under Inclusive Learning Environments. Captures the relational dimension of psychosocial wellbeing, whether young people feel they have trusted peers in the programme context.

Descriptor: Covers whether the programme is actively seeking to influence policy, regulation, or government practice through evidence submission, policy consultation participation, stakeholder engagement with government as the primary audience, and documented instances of government decision-making informed by programme evidence. The directional and strategic dimension of public sector engagement. Distinct from government relationship building, which is relational, and from adoption into public systems, which is the institutional outcome.

Distinct from adoption into public systems, which is the institutional outcome, and from policy influence and advocacy, which concerns changing government practice through evidence.

Descriptor: Covers whether learners have the foundational cognitive and language skills required to engage with learning at their current level, including language of instruction proficiency, working memory and attention, and pre-literacy and pre-numeracy readiness. Distinct from foundational learning experiences, which concerns current attainment, and from disability access and support, which concerns accommodation. Captures the cognitive prerequisites that determine whether a learner can access and process learning at their current level.
Indicators: How much do you agree with this statement: On most days when I arrive at my learning sessions, I feel mentally ready to concentrate and take in what is being taught. (https://www.notion.so/How-much-do-you-agree-with-this-statement-On-most-days-when-I-arrive-at-my-learning-sessions-I-fee-34060bfb014e818ba487ed2b8f8f0208?pvs=21), How much do you agree with this statement: When I sit down to learn, I am usually able to put aside worries or distractions from home and focus on the session. Descriptor: Covers whether learners consistently arrive at learning sessions on time, adequately prepared, and in a state of readiness to engage. The session-level behavioural readiness dimension distinct from attendance and enrolment continuity, which captures structural participation across the year. Captures punctuality, session preparedness, and alertness at session start as conditions that determine whether enrolled and present learners can benefit from each individual learning opportunity.

Descriptor: Covers whether teachers and facilitators have adequate motivation, working conditions, and wellbeing to sustain high-quality instructional practice over time. The human sustainability construct for the teaching workforce. Distinct from instructional leadership, which is the institutional enabling condition, and from professional learning cultures, which is collaborative learning. Captures the individual and relational conditions that determine whether teachers can and will sustain high-quality practice.
Indicators: How much do you agree with this statement: I believe that my teaching makes a real difference to how much my learners learn and progress. Descriptor: Covers whether school leaders and programme managers are providing the instructional leadership, structured teacher support, and school improvement planning required to create the institutional conditions for sustained learning quality improvement. The top-down enabling condition for school improvement. Distinct from professional learning cultures, which is peer-driven, and from assessment for learning systems, which is classroom-level. Captures whether leaders create the structural and cultural conditions that enable all other improvement processes to function.
Indicators: How much do you agree with this statement: My school leader or programme manager genuinely takes an interest in how I teach and how my learners are progressing.
